title: Scherenschnitte folded silhouette
subtitle: The German folded paper cutting technique for symmetrical designs
excerpt: Scherenschnitte, the German paper cutting tradition, produces perfectly symmetrical silhouettes by drawing and cutting through multiple folded layers simultaneously. A design drawn on one-quarter or one-eighth of the folded sheet appears in full when unfolded, reflected across each fold axis. The tradition uses black paper cut against white mounting, or white paper cut against dark backing.
sourceNotes: Scherenschnitte is documented as a folk craft tradition in German-speaking regions from at least the seventeenth century. Public-domain craft records and folk art histories describe the technique in detail. The folded-symmetry method described here is the classical home practice.

content.text: relies on a principle: anything cut through a folded sheet appears reflected across the

when opened. Fold once and the design is mirrored horizontally. Fold twice and the design appears four times, rotated around a centre point. This means a beginner only needs to draw and cut one-quarter of the final design and the symmetry is automatic. The critical skill is cutting the
cleanly: smooth cuts, no jagged edges, no torn corners.

attrs.body: Always cut away from fingers, keep the blade at a low angle to the mat, and replace blades when they drag rather than slice cleanly.
attrs.title: Craft knife safety

items.name: Black or coloured paper, 80 to 100 gsm, not too thin
Craft knife with a fresh blade
White or cream mounting card for the finished piece

content.text: Fold an A5 sheet in half to A6. Crease firmly. Fold in half again to make a quarter-size rectangle. Crease firmly. The folded stack is now four layers thick. Work with this folded sheet; all cuts go through all four layers simultaneously. The two folded edges are the fold axes; do not cut through a fold axis or the design will fall into separate pieces when unfolded.

Draw your design in pencil on the top layer of the folded sheet. For a first attempt, keep the design simple: a heart, a tree, a snowflake shape. Draw shapes that will be cut away from the interior of the folded piece, leaving a connected frame around the outside. Every shape you draw must be entirely enclosed by paper on all sides, if a cut shape reaches the outer edge of the paper, the piece will fall apart when unfolded. Leave at least 5 mm of paper between any cut shape and the outer edges or the fold lines.

Cut all interior shapes with a craft knife, working on the cutting mat. Cut curves with small, smooth strokes rather than one long sweep; the folded layers resist curves slightly. Cut points by entering from two directions and meeting at the apex, rather than trying to cut through the point in a single stroke. Once all interior cuts are made, use scissors to cut the outer silhouette edge of the folded piece if the design has a shaped outer boundary. Unfold carefully: the paper may be stiff from compression and the cut shapes can snag on each other.

Press the unfolded silhouette flat under a book for ten minutes. Apply glue stick to the back of the silhouette in small spots (full coverage will show through thin paper). Lay it onto the mounting card and press firmly. The cut paper may have small imperfections visible only when held to the light; these are invisible once mounted.

items.fix: Before cutting, trace your pencil lines and check that every cut shape is fully enclosed by remaining paper. No cut should reach the edge or cross a fold line.
items.cause: A cut line reached the outer edge of the paper or crossed a fold axis, disconnecting sections of the design.
items.symptom: The design falls into separate pieces when unfolded

items.fix: Replace the blade. Cut through folded layers requires a sharp blade and repeated light strokes rather than one heavy pull.
items.cause: The blade is blunt, or too much pressure is being applied in a single stroke.
items.symptom: The cut edges are ragged or torn rather than clean
